# Movable-Type Printing

Movable-type printing assembles a page from individual reusable characters that can
be arranged, inked, pressed onto paper, and then rearranged for the next page. In
fifteenth-century Europe, Johannes Gutenberg combined movable metal type with an
oil-based ink and a press adapted from winemaking, making books far faster and
cheaper to produce.

## Its impact

By slashing the cost of copying text, printing let ideas spread quickly and widely,
fuelling literacy, science and religious reform. Earlier movable type existed in East
Asia using clay and wood, but the metal-type press scaled especially well for
alphabetic scripts. The flood of printed books is often counted among the most
consequential changes in the history of communication.
